title: Landscape Architectural Services

narrative.executive_view: Landscape Architectural Services has real AI leverage in research, reports, concepts and RFP work, but the sector is still experimenting rather than operating AI as a mature production system. Current acquisition evidence shows strategic buyer interest, not a liquid small-firm platform market. The investable core is licensed, accountable site work; discretionary design and principal-held relationships make the rest pressured. [S1][S2][S9]
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The best directly observed landscape-architecture evidence is task-specific: ASLA reports AI use in writing reports (45%), conceptual design (41%) and RFP responses (29%), while IFLA reports research/writing/predesign as the leading uses. That supports partial automation of documentation and visualization, not autonomous stamping, agency coordination, site observation or design accountability. The supplied 5413 occupation mix remains an engineering-heavy proxy. [S1][S2]
narrative.value_capture: ASLA's standard forms explicitly contemplate both a landscape architect-client agreement and a landscape architect-consultant agreement. That establishes two economically different routes to market but does not quantify their revenue mix. I therefore treat retention as a bounded judgment: direct-client firms can retain some early productivity, whereas consultant work and competitive proposals can reprice it. Licensure protects who may perform regulated work in practice-act jurisdictions, not the price of every design output. [S3][S4][S5]
narrative.adoption_timing: I define effective adoption as routine applied use that has produced a reported workflow benefit, rather than any use in teaching, research or experimentation. In ASLA's more-than-300-response 2024 survey, 55% reported some AI use but only 27% reported time saved; the respondent pool was mixed, with 51% at landscape-architecture firms, and only 15% of firms allowed billable experimentation. I select 25% effective adoption after excluding the broad-use and experimental component, and estimate four years to 50%; this is a transparent mapping, not an observed forecast. [S1]
narrative.consolidation_economics: There are three directly documented U.S. buyer/target transactions inside the 24 months before this run: Greenprint acquired Omni Workshop (January 2025), ISG acquired Lift Environmental Design (July 2025), and Populous acquired OJB Landscape Architecture (May 2026). Populous had disclosed a Providence Equity Partners minority investment before the OJB deal; I do not infer PE backing for the other buyers. Consideration and target EBITDA are undisclosed, so the entry and exit multiples are deliberately bounded screening estimates anchored only loosely to ROG's mixed A/E transaction distribution. [S6][S7][S8][S9][S10]
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is PRESSURED, not melting. ASLA states that title acts restrict the title while practice acts restrict unlicensed performance of the work, and CLARB notes overlapping or incidental practice with architecture, engineering, interior design and surveying. This supplies an accessible, authoritative explanation for why accountable regulated work can persist while discretionary or adjacent-scope work remains contestable. No source located here quantifies the fee pool inside that protected perimeter, so this is not treated as a measured moat. [S3][S4]
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The core uncertainties are the contaminated 5413 role mix, the absence of a landscape-specific effective-adoption time series, unknown direct-client versus consultant revenue mix, undisclosed consideration in the three landscape deals, and no reopened owner-age/succession dataset. The score therefore gives no owner-age or succession bonus and uses low-confidence, explicitly bounded valuation estimates rather than importing unsupported A/E precision. [S1][S6][S7][S9][S10]

sources.title: How Landscape Architects Are Incorporating Artificial Intelligence
sources.what_it_supports: ASLA's 2024 survey reports the respondent mix, 55% any AI use, task uses, 27% reporting time saved, and 15% of firms allowing billable experimentation.

sources.title: 2025 AI in Landscape Architecture Survey
sources.what_it_supports: IFLA's global landscape-architecture survey reports AI use concentrated in research, writing and predesign, plus efficiency and governance findings.

sources.title: ASLA: Will I Need a License?
sources.what_it_supports: ASLA's accessible current page distinguishes title acts from practice acts and states that all 50 states license landscape architects.

sources.title: CLARB Glossary of Terms
sources.what_it_supports: CLARB defines practice acts and explains practice overlap and incidental practice among related design professions.

sources.title: ASLA Standard Form Contracts for Professional Services
sources.what_it_supports: ASLA offers distinct landscape architect-client and landscape architect-consultant forms, supporting the existence of both relationship types but not their revenue shares.

sources.title: Greenprint Acquires Omni Workshop, Landscape Architecture Studio
sources.what_it_supports: Greenprint announced its January 2025 acquisition of Omni Workshop, described as a full-service landscape architecture and design studio.

sources.title: ISG Acquires Lift Environmental Design
sources.what_it_supports: ISG announced its July 2025 acquisition of Lift Environmental Design, a landscape architecture and planning firm.

sources.title: Populous Announces Minority Investment from Providence Equity Partners
sources.what_it_supports: Populous disclosed a 2023 strategic minority investment from Providence Equity Partners and an intent to expand its services and geography.

sources.title: Populous Acquires OJB Landscape Architecture
sources.what_it_supports: Populous announced its May 2026 acquisition of OJB Landscape Architecture; the announcement identifies landscape architecture, urban design and public-realm capabilities but discloses no consideration or EBITDA.

sources.title: ROG: Latest A/E Business Valuation and M&A Transaction Study
sources.what_it_supports: ROG reports 257 mixed architecture, engineering and environmental transactions and their all-firms EV/EBITDA distribution; it is an explicitly non-landscape-specific valuation reference.

labor_share.method: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541320 ($2,653,798,071) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541320 ($5,788,521,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n_band.derivation: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $206,548 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541320); class <5: 3,549 firms, avg revenue/firm $379,051 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $59,321 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 800 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,193,496 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $186,782 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 390 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,621,638 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $410,286 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 256 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,121,246 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $957,975 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $23,646,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,700,651 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,128,077 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,428,544 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 46
n_band.margin_source: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Observed use is concentrated in early design, reports and RFPs, so production/documentation roles receive partial rather than majority automation fractions; accountable field and management work remains low. The 4-digit occupation mix is a disclosed proxy.
caveats: The supplied 5413 role mix is engineering-dominated rather than 541320-specific.
Survey use is task- and respondent-based, not a measured labor-hour replacement study.

breakdown_by_role.rationale: Reports, concepts and documentation are partly AI-assisted; licensed engineering judgment, approvals and site responsibility remain human. [S1][S2]
Conceptual and report work is AI-assisted, but accountable design and client judgment are not shown as automated. [S1][S2]
RFP, report and coordination documentation can be assisted; client and agency coordination persists. [S1][S2]
Production and visualization have more exposure, but the cited surveys show early design and concepts rather than autonomous construction-document output. [S1][S2]
Administrative drafting is assisted, while operating decisions and client relationships remain human. [S1]
Documentation assistance is plausible, but technical accountability and coordination remain human. [S1][S2]
AI can assist reports and proposals, not managerial responsibility or professional accountability. [S1]
Physical inspection and site observation have little direct support for replacement in the cited task evidence. [S1][S2]
Some documentation and analytical assistance is plausible; licensed technical work remains human. [S1][S2]
Tooling can accelerate coding tasks, but the source does not support full replacement. [S1]
Unlisted wage share likely contains mixed design support and administration; selected below a majority because observed use remains early-stage. [S1][S2]

pi_dist.rationale: Direct-client and consultant contracting routes coexist; I select 40% retained savings because direct relationships can retain some productivity while consultant work and competitive proposals reprice it. No cited source quantifies the mix.
caveats: ASLA contract forms demonstrate relationship types, not fee method, price realization or revenue shares.

pi_moat.rationale: Practice acts can protect licensed performance, but title acts and overlapping/incidental practice limit the breadth of the moat. The selected value does not assume a state count or a measured regulated-fee share.
caveats: No accessible source in this packet quantifies 541320 revenue inside the regulated practice perimeter.

t50_years.rationale: At the selected 25% effective baseline, the survey's 77% trial-and-error learning and 15% billable-experimentation permission indicate that routine, value-realizing firm deployment is still immature; four years to 50% is a bounded forward estimate.
caveats: No landscape-specific longitudinal effective-adoption series was located; this is not derived from a CAD/BIM timeline.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Effective adoption is mapped to routine applied use with a reported workflow benefit: 27% reported time saved, versus 55% reporting any use across practice, teaching or research. I select 25% after discounting the mixed respondent pool and experimental use.
caveats: The ASLA survey is not a census of US 541320 firms and its time-saved measure is respondent-reported.

historical_analogs.value: No landscape-architecture-specific CAD/BIM adoption series was validated. The adoption estimate instead uses the current survey distinction between broad AI use and reported time saving; no historical technology cadence is imported.
historical_analogs.rationale: The current task and workflow observations are relevant to adoption maturity; they do not substantiate a BIM/CAD historical analog.
caveats: No dated landscape-specific CAD/BIM adoption observations were reopened.

owners_60plus_pct.rationale: No owner-level age evidence was reopened, so this remediation assigns no owner-age bonus rather than carrying forward an unsupported estimate.
caveats: Zero is a scoring-conservative placeholder, not a claim that no owners are over 60.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No accessible landscape-architecture succession survey was reopened; no succession-shortage bonus is applied.
caveats: Absence of evidence in this packet is not evidence of no succession issue.

active_consolidators.rationale: Three distinct buyers announced acquisitions of explicitly landscape-architecture targets within the 24 months preceding 2026-07-21: Greenprint/Omni, ISG/Lift and Populous/OJB.
active_consolidators.plausible_range: 3 observed named buyer/target transactions; not a census of consolidators
caveats: The announcements do not disclose target EBITDA, consideration, recurring-platform strategy or PE backing for every buyer; only Populous' separate PE minority investment is verified in S8.

buy_mult.rationale: No consideration or target EBITDA is disclosed for the three landscape transactions. I use 3.5x as a conservative screening entry estimate near the 3.35x lower quartile of ROG's mixed all-firm A/E sample, not as an observed landscape transaction multiple.
caveats: ROG covers mixed architecture, engineering and environmental transactions, including internal minority interests; it is not a 541320 target-comps series.

exit_mult.rationale: Three current strategic landscape deals and the Populous/OJB buyer-capital evidence support buyer interest, but not a price. I select 4.5x, below ROG's 5.30x all-firms median and within its 3.35x–5.38x all-firms range, as a bounded exit screening estimate.
caveats: No cited landscape-architecture transaction reports a paid multiple or establishes an eligible $10M-plus EBITDA platform exit.

pricing_structure.value: The accessible evidence confirms both direct landscape architect-client and landscape architect-consultant contractual routes; their fee mix is unmeasured in this packet.
pricing_structure.rationale: Use the verified contract categories to describe route-to-market heterogeneity and avoid claiming an unsupported lump-sum, hourly or sub-consultant share.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Relationship types observed; revenue mix unknown
caveats: No accessible LA-specific billing or prime/sub-consultant revenue-share survey was reopened.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: A 25% effective-adoption baseline and four-year t50 are consistent with task use concentrated in early workflow stages and only 27% reporting time saved; the labor uplift is theoretical rather than a current realized saving. [S1][S2]

terminal_value.justification: Accessible ASLA and CLARB evidence supports licensed practice protection and overlapping/incidental adjacent practice, respectively. This preserves accountable work but does not establish a protected revenue share; discretionary work is therefore pressured. [S3][S4]

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No: the supplied 4-digit 5413 occupation mix is engineering-contaminated and does not represent a code-specific landscape-architecture wage mix.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Relationship types are verified, but their revenue mix is not. pi_dist is a conservative estimate that allows direct-client retention and consultant/competitive pass-through without claiming a sourced billing split. [S5]

dataset_mismatch.description: The frozen 4-digit 5413 role mix omits landscape architects from its top-ten occupations and is engineering-dominated; automation fractions are therefore cautious task estimates, not code-specific labor measurements.
sensitivity: A code-specific landscape-architect/designer/admin wage mix could materially change V.
A measured direct-client versus consultant mix could change C.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Professional site planning and landscape design
market_boundary.selection_basis: The score evaluates US 541320 professional services, excluding contractor installation economics while recognizing adjacent architecture, engineering and design-build competition.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A faster conversion of early-stage AI use into routine time-saving work raises A and accelerates realized V.
Undisclosed landscape-deal pricing makes M a bounded screening estimate, not a transaction-comps conclusion.

reviewer_note: Attempt 2 remediates the prior fleet findings: the inaccessible exemptions PDF is replaced by reopened ASLA and CLARB pages; effective adoption is explicitly mapped from broad use to reported workflow benefit; three named landscape-architecture deals and one verified PE-backed buyer are supplied; unsupported CAD/BIM, owner-age, succession, billing-mix and deal-price claims are removed or bounded as estimates.

A.arithmetic: Python v3.1.2: A = 10 when t50=0 else min(10,10/t50); t50=4, A=2.5
